content: {"sections": [{"title": "Background, Definitions, and Protocols", "description": "Defines intermittent fasting and its principal protocols (16:8 time-restricted eating, 5:2, alternate-day fasting, fasting-mimicking diets). Distinguishes IF as a timing-based intervention from continuous energy restriction. Establishes why the benefits vs. energy-restriction question is the central analytic axis.", "search_keywords": "intermittent fasting definition, time-restricted eating, 16:8, alternate-day fasting, 5:2, fasting-mimicking diet, continuous energy restriction", "evidence_summary": "IF encompasses 16:8 TRE, ADF, 5:2, and FMD protocols; the scientific question is whether effects exceed matched CER (Varady, Scientific). Commercial ecosystem markets these protocols ahead of evidence base (Haub, Industry).", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Industry"], "order": 1}, {"title": "Weight Loss Efficacy vs Continuous Energy Restriction", "description": "Synthesizes RCT and meta-analytic evidence comparing IF to CER for weight loss under matched-energy-intake conditions. Examines magnitude, duration, and whether fasting confers benefits beyond energy balance.", "search_keywords": "intermittent fasting RCT weight loss, isoenergetic, continuous energy restriction, Harvie, Lowe 2020, Templeman 2021, meta-analysis", "evidence_summary": "Multiple SRs and RCTs find IF and CER produce similar weight loss under matched energy intake (Varady). Lowe 2020 TREAT found no significant between-group weight loss for 16:8 TRE vs CMT (-0.26 kg, P=.63). Templeman 2021 showed ADF inferior to matched CER for fat loss. An outlier paper claims CR produced no weight loss, contradicting the preponderance of evidence.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "order": 2}, {"title": "Cardiometabolic Biomarkers and Glycemic Control", "description": "Reviews evidence on whether IF improves fasting glucose, insulin, HbA1c, lipids, and blood pressure beyond what energy balance alone delivers. Incorporates findings on T2DM safety and glycemic variability under short-duration TRE.", "search_keywords": "HbA1c, fasting glucose, HOMA-IR, LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, blood pressure, T2DM, time-restricted eating safety", "evidence_summary": "Lowe 2020 found no significant within- or between-group differences in glucose, insulin, HbA1c, lipids, or BP (Varady). Templeman 2021: no differences in postprandial cardiometabolic indices or gut hormones between fasting and matched CER (Varady). TRE in T2DM appeared safe short-term with glucose monitoring (Zeratsky).", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Public_Health"], "order": 3}, {"title": "Body Composition and Lean Mass Safety Signal", "description": "Examines whether IF differentially affects fat mass vs lean mass. Highlights the appendicular lean mass loss signal from TREAT and contrasting TRE findings where lean mass was preserved.", "search_keywords": "appendicular lean mass, DXA, sarcopenia, fat mass, body composition, TRE lean mass loss", "evidence_summary": "Lowe 2020: TRE caused significant appendicular lean mass loss (-0.64 kg, P<.001) with significant between-group difference (-0.47 kg, P=.009) favoring CMT (Varady). Chow 2020 found TRE did not cause trunk/arm lean mass loss \u2014 direct contradiction requiring careful interpretation.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "order": 4}, {"title": "Real-World Adherence, Retention, and Commercial App Evidence", "description": "Reviews real-world adherence and retention data from commercial IF apps (Zero, Simple), compares these to trial-based effect sizes, and examines the gap between marketing claims and peer-reviewed magnitudes.", "search_keywords": "fasting app, Zero app, Simple app, retention, adherence, real-world weight loss, marketing claims vs RCT", "evidence_summary": "Torres 2022 52-week observational app study: users lost >1% starting weight per Eligible Fasting Hour per day by week 26 (Haub). Huntriss 2024: SIMPLE app engagement directly associated with weight loss (Haub). No direct head-to-head effect-size comparisons between app-user cohorts and RCT arms exist. Marketing sources cite individual-level 30-100 pound losses not representative of cohort averages.", "perspectives": ["Industry", "Scientific"], "order": 5}, {"title": "Safety, Contraindications, and Populations to Avoid IF", "description": "Synthesizes population-specific contraindications: pregnancy, lactation, insulin/sulfonylurea users, and populations with T1DM or eating-disorder history. Characterizes evidence strength for each contraindication.", "search_keywords": "pregnancy fasting, lactation breastfeeding, sulfonylurea hypoglycemia, insulin-user, eating disorder, adolescent fasting, shift worker", "evidence_summary": "IF in pregnancy: minor but significant neonatal birth-weight reduction and lower placental weight (Zeratsky). Lactation: preclinical undernutrition in offspring persistent to weaning; clinical guidance advises against (Zeratsky). Sulfonylurea users: documented severe hypoglycemia cases; drug class at intrinsic hypoglycemia risk (Zeratsky). Evidence gaps for adolescents, shift workers, and eating-disorder history.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health"], "order": 6}, {"title": "Regulatory Frameworks for Health Claim Substantiation", "description": "Describes FDA Authorized vs Qualified Health Claim tiers, EFSA disease-risk-reduction criteria, and the gap between current IF evidence and claim-substantiation thresholds. Notes absence of IF-specific agency opinions.", "search_keywords": "FDA SSA, qualified health claim, 21 CFR Part 101 Subpart E, EFSA NDA, Regulation EC 1924/2006, health claim substantiation", "evidence_summary": "FDA SSA standard requires 'significant scientific agreement' codified in 21 CFR 101 Subpart E (MacNeill). Qualified Health Claim tier allows disclaimer-conditioned claims below SSA (MacNeill). EFSA disease-risk-reduction claims governed by Regulation EC 1924/2006 (MacNeill). No IF-specific SSA, QHC, or EFSA opinion surfaced.", "perspectives": ["Regulatory"], "order": 7}, {"title": "Cost-Effectiveness and Health-Economic Implications", "description": "Reviews available cost-utility analyses relevant to IF and compares to structural lifestyle interventions and pharmacotherapy. Identifies opportunity-cost and head-to-head-comparison gaps.", "search_keywords": "cost-effectiveness, QALY, ICER, fasting-mimicking diet, GLP-1 semaglutide comparison, NICE, Markov model, DPP", "evidence_summary": "One T2DM FMD cost-utility analysis exists (trial + lifetime model) but specific ICERs not captured in snippets (Galvani). Non-pharmacologic diet/exercise QALY costs range $25,400\u2013$57,400 (Galvani). NEW Soul Study: $1,704\u2013$7,889/QALY (Galvani). NICE dietary intervention: \u00a316,794/QALY. No head-to-head IF vs GLP-1 analysis exists.", "perspectives": ["Economic"], "order": 8}]}
